items.author: https://www.chinatalk.media/feed
items.title: Sen. Slotkin: NDAA, AI guardrails, and banning China's cars
items.textEn: of Michigan, who served in OSD Policy and three terms in the House before joining the Senate Armed Services Committee, joins ChinaTalk to break down what got in, what got voted down, and why NDAA markup days are the only two days a year the Senate acts like a functioning institution. Beyond the NDAA, we also discuss… The AI Guardrails Act, the Anthropic debate, and why no one SecWar or AI company should set the rules for the kill chain, Her bill with Bernie Moreno banning Chinese connected vehicles, The Democratic playbook if the party flips a chamber in November, Data ownership, the Midwest’s data center revolt, and why a healthy democracy would be talking about AI every single day. Listen now on your favorite podcast app . NDAA Markup—The Most Fun to Be Had in the Senate Jordan Schneider: Senator Slotkin of Michigan, formerly of OSD Policy and the House of Representatives — welcome to ChinaTalk. Senator Elissa Slotkin: Thanks for having me. Jordan Schneider: Let’s start with the new NDAA we have, sort of. What are you excited and concerned about? Let’s talk in particular about ROBOCOM, the robotic and autonomous systems combatant command that the committee was excited to kick off. Senator Slotkin: Look, the two days we do the NDAA markup in the Senate are typically my best days of the year. They are behind closed doors — which is probably why people act more normal — and there’s real substantive debate on provisions. Most importantly, say a Republican colleague has a provision and I’m like, "Look, I’d be with you if you just changed X, Y, and Z." They send their staff back to change X, Y, and Z, and three hours later we revisit it, it gets a vote, and it gets in. Or two people have similar amendments on different sides of the aisle, and they go compromise over lunch and fix it. That doesn’t happen enough in this place, so I’m always happy to be in NDAA markup. Jordan Schneider: Maybe before we do the specific stuff — is there any structural change that would make more of the building work like SASC does on NDAA markup days? Senator Slotkin: The way you would do it is probably never gonna happen, because it means you kick the cameras out. That’s the only difference — the cameras are not there, so people don’t feel like they need to play to an audience. They aren’t kicking back with their political talking points; they’re actually doing substantive work. And it’s a pretty hard argument to make: "Hey America, we’re going to be less transparent in the Senate." That’s a hard thing to do. I think there’s a million ways to make the Senate more efficient. The Senate was a caterpillar and could one day be a butterfly, but is right now in the disgusting cocoon and doesn’t know what it’s going to be. I have a million ideas on how to reform this institution. But we’re effective in the NDAA because people are not playing to the bleachers. Jordan Schneider: Do people really need those clips, though? Senator Slotkin: I don’t know that people wake up in the morning and say, "I’m so excited for my five minutes in front of an Armed Services hearing today." But those hearings have been televised for over twenty years, and no one’s gonna be the chairman who says, "I’m kicking out the public," right? And I don’t think the public feels like they want less oversight and insight into what the House and Senate are doing. So it’s a tough position. The way to deal with this is a different vision of how we use these committees — how we do work, how we build coalitions around big ideas. You were just talking about the robotics command. That’s a big deal — the idea to pass something that would potentially be a new command — and unfortunately, we don’t start discussing it until we’re really in markup. Our staff are looking at it before then, for sure, but you could imagine different ways of doing these things. I’m open to all of that. But for the reasons I described, I like NDAA markup because pe…
items.summaryZh: 密歇根州参议员埃莉萨·斯洛特金在接受 ChinaTalk 访谈时谈及国防授权法案（NDAA）审议的独特氛围、对机器人与自主作战指挥部（ROBOCOM）的期待及其在闭门讨论中取得的实质性进展，并指出该流程因缺乏公开镜头而更专注于内容，这对投资者了解国防预算走向和相关技术公司潜在政策支持具有重要参考价值。
items.translationZh:
标题：参议员斯洛特金：NDAA、AI 守则以及禁止中国汽车
正文：密歇根州参议员埃莉萨·斯洛特金（曾在国防部政策局任职并在众议院服务三届，后加入参议院军备服务委员会）加入 ChinaTalk，拆解哪些内容进入法案、哪些被否决，以及为何 NDAA 标记日是参议院一年中仅有的两天能够像一个有效机构运作的时段。除 NDAA 外，我们还讨论…《AI 守则法案》、Anthropic（人工智能公司）争议，以及为何任何国防部或 AI 公司都不应制定杀伤链规则；她与伯尼·莫雷诺共同推动的禁止中国关联车辆的法案；如果民主党在十一月翻转议院的选举策略手册；数据所有权、中西部数据中心的反抗，以及为何一个健康的民主社会应每日讨论 AI。现在就可在您喜欢的播客应用收听。
NDAA 标记——参议院最有趣的时刻
乔丹·施耐德：密歇根州参议员斯洛特金，曾在国防部政策局和众议院任职——欢迎来到 ChinaTalk。
参议员埃莉萨·斯洛特金：感谢邀请。
乔丹·施耐德：让我们先从我们现在拥有的新 NDAA 开始谈起。你对哪些内容感到兴奋或担忧？特别是我们委员会很期待启动的 ROBOCOM——机器人与自主系统作战司令部。
参议员斯洛特金：看，参议院进行 NDAA 标记的两天通常是我一年中最好的日子。这些讨论在闭门进行——这可能是人们行为更正常的原因——并且对条款进行实质性辩论。最重要的是，如果一位共和党同僚提出一个条款，我会说，‘只要你把 X、Y、Z 改一下，我就支持。’他们的工作人员回来修改 X、Y、Z，三小时后我们再次审议，通过投票，这条款就进了法案。或者两位议员在不同党派提出类似的修正案，他们在午餐时达成妥协并共同完善。这种情况在这里并不常见，所以我总是很高兴参加 NDAA 标记。
乔丹·施耐德：在具体议题之前，是否有任何结构性改革可以让更多事务像 NDAA 标记日那样高效运作？
参议员斯洛特金：要做到这点几乎不可能，因为这意味着要把摄像机赶走。唯一的区别就是——没有摄像机，人们就不觉得需要对观众表演。他们不再围绕政治口号，而是实质性工作。而且这很难说服公众：‘美国，我们要在参议院降低透明度。’这很难。我认为有无数方法可以让参议院更高效。参议院曾是毛毛虫，可能有一天会变成蝴蝶，但现在正处在令人不适的茧中，还不知道会变成什么。我对改革机构有千百个想法。但我们在 NDAA 上之所以有效，是因为人们不在为观众表演。
乔丹·施耐德：但人们真的需要那些录像吗？
参议员斯洛特金：我不确定人们早上醒来会说，‘我为今天在武装部队听证会上出现的五分钟感到兴奋。’但这些听证会已经电视直播二十多年了，没有人会是那位说‘我要把公众赶出去’的主席，我也不认为公众希望对众议院和参议院的工作失去监督和了解。因此，这是一种艰难的平衡。解决办法是对委员会的工作方式有不同的设想——我们如何开展工作，如何围绕大理念建立联盟。你刚才提到机器人指挥部，这是件大事——通过可能新设立的指挥部的想法。但遗憾的是，我们直到真正进入标记阶段才开始讨论。我们的工作人员在此之前已经在研究，但你可以想象有不同的做事方式。我对所有这些都持开放态度。但正如我所描述的原因，我喜欢 NDAA 标记，因为……

items.author: https://www.chinatalk.media/feed
items.title: Paul Kennedy on Great Powers
items.textEn: What a profound honor to have Paul Kennedy on the ChinaTalk podcast. Kennedy is my favorite living historian and the writer who’s most shaped my intellectual development. His analysis underpins what you hear on this show every week. The Rise and Fall of the Great Powers is an epochal work that traces global power transitions from 1500 to the present. It’s gripping, forest-and-trees scholarship at its finest. Equally impressive in different ways is his book, The Rise of Anglo-German Antagonism, 1860 to 1914 . Not only is it god-tier diplomatic history, but it also gives you a feel for the era through its explorations of social, economic, domestic, political, and cultural dimensions of Anglo-German relations. There are fascinating US/China analogies that we’ll get into in this podcast. His two most recent works directly inform the military coverage on ChinaTalk. Engineers of Victory looks at how people and the systems they worked within solved engineering challenges that turned the tide for entire theaters in World War II. His latest, Victory at Sea: Naval Power and the Transformation of Global Order in World War II , is a sweeping history of a radical transformation in the balance of military power, from the mid-1930s when America was just gaining prominence, to after World War II, when it had no other significant naval competitor. The Parliament of Man: A History of the United Nations made me interested in international organizations and gave me my senior thesis topic about the creation of the UN. What Kennedy taught me more than anything is this — sweat the details, look at the individual players, and zoom out often enough to understand what truly shapes the long-term fate of nations. Over the course of this episode, we pick up themes from all across his work: Great Power rivalries of the late 19th-early 20th centuries, the China echoes today, and why potential antagonisms turn nice and others turn belligerent, The persistent struggles of liberal internationalists and why they rarely get the outcomes they want, Why China today is not Germany of the late 19th and early 20th centuries, The surprising ways geography shapes global power dynamics, How fear spreads among nations and why mutual suspicion is so hard to escape, Why top powers blow it and lose their dominant place in the world, How systems and innovation win wars. And much more, including salutary lessons from the Dutch and Swedes on boring yet prosperous futures, how Churchill’s interest in gadgets influenced the course of the Second World War, and why transformative action from the UN remains unlikely in the near future. Listen now on your favorite podcast app . How to Win Friends and Alienate Nations Jordan Schneider: What is national power, and why does it matter? Paul Kennedy: Since the earliest struggles between tribes and later between larger groups of people with their symbols and more organized armies, and in particular, the emergence of the modern nation-state around the year 1500 (as historians roughly date it) — this entity has existed. The nation-state, with its capacity to raise taxes, conscript, and enroll men to fight under the direction of, say, a Dutch republic or a Spanish monarchy, has fundamentally shaped global affairs. Since that era, with the rise of the nation-state in Western Europe and its subsequent adoption worldwide, this thing we call "the country," "the nation," or "the state," has operated within what political scientists term an anarchic international system. "Anarchy" here doesn’t necessarily mean everyone slaughtering everyone else, but simply the absence of a higher authority controlling global affairs. The United Nations General Assembly lists approximately 189 or 192 nation-states, large and small. We give them all a membership, a vote in the General Assembly. Almost all of them have some sort of diplomatic service as well as a domestic home service, have a government and a constitution of one form or another, and inte…
items.summaryZh: 本文介绍了历史学家Paul Kennedy的主要著作及其在ChinaTalk播客中的讨论，强调其对大国兴衰、国际秩序和美中关系的深刻洞见，对投资者了解地缘政治走势与长期国家竞争格局具有重要参考价值。
items.translationZh:
标题：Paul Kennedy谈大国

正文：能够请到Paul Kennedy参加ChinaTalk播客，我感到无比荣幸。Kennedy是我最喜爱的在世历史学家，也是对我的思想发展影响最大的作者。他的分析是本节目每周内容的基石。《大国的兴衰》是一部划时代的著作，追溯了自1500年至今的全球权力转移过程。它引人入胜，是宏观与微观研究的极致展现。同样令人印象深刻的是他的另一部作品《英德对抗的崛起，1860‑1914》，这不仅是顶级的外交史著作，还通过对英德关系的社会、经济、国内、政治和文化层面的探讨，让读者感受到那个时代的氛围。书中有许多关于美中类比的有趣之处，我们将在本期播客中深入探讨。他的两部最新著作直接为ChinaTalk的军事专题提供了信息。《胜利的工程师》研究了二战中人们及其所处体系如何解决工程难题，从而扭转整个战区的局势。最新的《海上胜利：海军力量与二战全球秩序的转变》则是一部宏大的历史，描绘了从20世纪30年代中期美国刚刚崭露头角，到二战结束后美国已无其他显著的海军竞争者，军事力量平衡的根本转变。《人类议会：联合国史》让我对国际组织产生兴趣，并成为我关于联合国创建的高级论文课题。Kennedy教给我最重要的一点是——关注细节，考察个体行动者，同时又要经常拉大视角，才能真正把握决定民族长期命运的因素。在本期节目中，我们将从他的全部著作中抽取主题：19世纪末‑20世纪初的大国竞争、当今中国的回响、为何有的潜在敌对会转为友好而有的则演变为敌对、自由主义国际主义者的持续斗争以及他们为何常常难以实现预期目标、为何当今的中国并非19、20世纪末的德国、地理如何意外地塑造全球权力动态、恐惧如何在国家间蔓延以及相互猜疑为何难以摆脱、为何顶级大国会犯错误并失去世界主导地位、系统与创新如何决定战争胜负，等等。我们还将探讨荷兰和瑞典在平凡却繁荣的未来中的启示、丘吉尔对小工具的兴趣如何影响二战进程，以及为何联合国的变革行动在短期内仍不太可能实现。立即在您喜欢的播客应用上收听。

如何赢得朋友并疏远国家
Jordan Schneider：什么是国家实力，为什么它重要？
Paul Kennedy：自部落之间的最早冲突以及后来更大群体之间的冲突——他们拥有符号和更组织化的军队，尤其是大约在1500年左右（历史学家大致如此划分）出现的现代民族国家——这种实体就此存在。民族国家具备征税、征兵并在比如荷兰共和国或西班牙君主制的指挥下组织人们作战的能力，这根本塑造了全球事务。自那时起，随着民族国家在西欧的崛起并随后在全球范围内的推广，我们所谓的“国家”“民族”或“政体”就在政治学家所称的无政府国际体系（anarchic international system）中运作。这里的“无政府”并不一定意味着人人相互屠杀，而是指缺乏一个更高层级的权威来控制全球事务。联合国大会列出了大约189至192个国家，大大小小。我们给它们全部成员资格，在大会上拥有投票权。几乎所有国家都有某种形式的外交机构和国内行政机构，拥有政府和一定形式的宪法，且…
